Q1: Honestly, my study routine was chaotic at best. I tried to dedicate around 34 hours a week, but it was never organized the way I wanted it to be. I'd often find myself studying late into the night because I couldn't focus during the day with everything else going on. I made lists and schedules, but life kept throwing things at me, so I'd end up cramming whenever I could find a moment. Visual stuff like diagrams and charts helped me retain information, so I spent a lot of time creating those instead of just reading.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be consistent, studying at the same times, but then something would come up—work, family obligations, just life being difficult—and everything would shift. Deadlines definitely dictated when I studied most intensely. I wish I could say I had a regular schedule, but the truth is I was always reacting to whatever was most urgent at the moment.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an assignment or wanting to check if there was something I missed. I'd log in when I remembered there was a deadline coming up or when I got anxious about falling behind. Sometimes I'd just log in to watch the videos because that was the easiest way for me to absorb information visually. The anxiety was honestly a big driver—I'd wake up worried I'd missed something important and immediately check the LMS.
Q4: For a while, yeah, I got into a rhythm where I'd check the LMS every morning with my coffee. It became almost automatic, like checking my phone for messages. But that routine broke down when things got really stressful midway through the course. After that, it was more sporadic—I'd binge-use it for a few days, then not touch it for a while. The habit was there, but it wasn't sustainable with everything else I was dealing with.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me. I'm a visual learner, so being able to watch and re-watch explanations made concepts click in ways that reading never did. I spent close to an hour on average watching videos because I'd pause, rewind, and take visual notes. Any diagrams or infographics the instructors provided were gold. The discussion posts were less useful to me personally, but I tried to engage at least once.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. With limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading on a screen for hours hurt my eyes and made my anxiety worse. I also delayed the discussion forums because they felt time-consuming and I wasn't sure my contributions were valuable. When you're stretched thin, you have to prioritize, and those things just fell to the bottom of the list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. Visual content that helped me understand difficult concepts was always worth it. If something felt abstract or disconnected from what I needed to do to pass, I struggled to justify spending time on it. I also valued anything that reduced my anxiety about the course—like practice questions that showed me where I stood.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. When you're barely keeping up with required work, optional stuff becomes this guilt-inducing reminder of what you're not doing. Required activities had urgency attached to them, so I prioritized those even when I was exhausted. Sometimes I'd look at optional materials and think 'that would be helpful,' but then reality would hit and I'd focus on what was graded.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that never showed up in the LMS. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I had good internet access, then study them offline for hours. I made my own visual summaries and diagrams on paper because that's how I learn best. I also used YouTube videos and free resources online to supplement what I didn't understand. A lot of my real learning happened away from the platform.
Q10: There were many nights where I sat with my handwritten notes and diagrams, working through problems without touching the LMS at all. I'd print materials at the library when I could afford it and study from those physical copies. Group study sessions with peers happened in person, not through the platform. The LMS would show I was inactive, but I was actually deeply engaged with the course content in my own way.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video once, download it or take detailed visual notes, and then never go back to it on the LMS. My internet isn't always reliable, so downloading and working offline became my survival strategy. I'd watch a lecture once online, screenshot key diagrams, and then review those images repeatedly without logging back in. The LMS only saw that initial access, not the hours I spent with that material afterward.
Q12: Midway through the course, everything became harder. My anxiety spiked, I was dealing with financial stress, and I felt like I was drowning. There were weeks where just opening the LMS felt overwhelming because I knew how behind I was. The CBT sessions helped me cope, but they didn't make the workload disappear. When you're running on empty and worried about basic things, academic engagement becomes incredibly difficult.
Q13: All of those things affected me constantly. The workload felt impossible sometimes, and when I was confused about material, my anxiety would spiral. Fatigue was a constant companion because I was studying so many hours while dealing with everything else. I'd log into the LMS with good intentions but feel too mentally exhausted to actually engage meaningfully. Competing responsibilities—work, family—always seemed to win when I had to choose.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered to all my worries. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything because I was too anxious or tired to focus. I'd technically be 'active' on the LMS but completely checked out mentally. It's frustrating because I wanted to engage, but my brain just wouldn't cooperate.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety made starting things feel impossible—I'd look at an assignment and feel paralyzed by how much I didn't understand. I'd tell myself I needed to review more before attempting it, but that review time would disappear. Avoidance became a coping mechanism, even though it made everything worse in the end. The weight of incomplete tasks was always there, adding to my stress.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd have bursts of intense LMS activity right before something was due, cramming in videos and materials I should have engaged with earlier. Without a deadline looming, I struggled to make myself log in. It's not healthy, but the pressure of a deadline was often the only thing that could cut through my anxiety and get me moving. My engagement was very deadline-driven.
Q17: Feedback was a mixed bag for me. When I got positive feedback, it motivated me to engage more and reduced my anxiety temporarily. But negative or critical feedback sometimes sent me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for days. I wanted to improve based on feedback, but processing it emotionally took time. The delay in getting feedback also made it hard to adjust my approach in real-time.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and overwhelming. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me something to work toward, which I needed. But the sheer volume of content made it hard to keep up, especially with my situation. I appreciated when things were organized visually and logically. The structure helped me prioritize, but it also made it painfully obvious when I was falling behind, which increased my anxiety.
Q19: Visual materials and clearly organized modules made engagement easier for me. When content was presented in digestible chunks with diagrams, I could actually learn. What made it harder was when there was too much text-heavy content or when expectations weren't clear. The discussion requirements felt forced and added stress without adding much to my learning. More flexibility in how we could demonstrate understanding would have helped someone like me.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. You can be logging in constantly, watching videos, checking materials—all that activity—but still struggle to produce work. For me, it was anxiety and perfectionism. I'd engage with content but then freeze when it came to actually submitting something. Fear of failure, confusion about requirements, or just being too exhausted to complete the final step. Activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: That makes sense to me too. Some people are efficient—they know exactly what they need, grab it quickly, and work offline. Or they have strong foundational knowledge and don't need to engage as much with every resource. I tried to be that person by downloading things and studying offline, but my completion rates suffered anyway. Low LMS activity doesn't mean someone isn't learning; it might mean they've found other ways to engage.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on videos. That's useful data. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and everything happening offline. They can't capture the hours I spent with printed notes or the mental effort of working through anxiety to even log in. They also can't show why someone might be struggling or what's happening in their life outside the course.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and wrestling with the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're thinking about course concepts even when you're not studying, when you're genuinely trying to understand rather than just pass. For me, engagement also means showing up despite everything working against you. I was engaged even when my metrics looked bad—I cared deeply about learning, I just couldn't always show it in visible ways.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I was fighting just to stay afloat. It can't capture the anxiety attacks before logging in, or the hours of offline study, or the genuine desire to succeed despite limited resources. It doesn't show that I went to CBT sessions trying to manage my mental health so I could be a better student. My engagement was real and intense—it just didn't always translate into clicks and completions. The human struggle behind the data is invisible.
